arrivalSteps.title: Visa Requirements & MDAC
arrivalSteps.description: Most nationalities from Commonwealth, ASEAN, and major Western nations receive visa-free entry for 30–90 days. The Malaysia Digital Arrival Card (MDAC) at imigresen-online.imi.gov.my is mandatory for all foreign visitors and must be completed within 3 days before arrival.

arrivalSteps.title: Passport Control / Immigration
arrivalSteps.description: Proceed to the foreigners' immigration queue at KLIA, klia2, or other entry points. Present your passport and MDAC confirmation. Officers will check documentation, ask about purpose and duration of stay, and verify your accommodation address.

arrivalSteps.title: Biometrics
arrivalSteps.description: Fingerprints (both index fingers) are scanned at the immigration counter for all non-Malaysian citizens. A facial photograph is also taken. Biometric data is stored for future entries.

arrivalSteps.title: Baggage Claim
arrivalSteps.description: Collect your checked baggage from the designated carousel. Retain your baggage claim tags as they are checked by customs officers at the exit.

arrivalSteps.title: Customs Declaration
arrivalSteps.description: Duty-free allowance: goods up to MYR 500, 200 cigarettes or 225g tobacco, 1L alcohol (non-Muslim). Declare cash or monetary instruments over MYR 30,000. Prohibited: drugs (mandatory death penalty for trafficking), pornographic material, firearms without permit.

arrivalSteps.title: Exit to Arrivals Hall
arrivalSteps.description: After customs clearance, proceed to the arrivals hall. At KLIA, the KLIA Ekspres train provides fast access to Kuala Lumpur city centre (28 minutes). Registered taxis and e-hailing apps (Grab) are available from designated bays.

fallbackAlerts.message: Malaysia enforces the mandatory death penalty for drug trafficking. Even possession of small amounts can result in severe punishment.

scenarios.description: Mosque etiquette and dress code in Malaysia

arrivalSteps.title: Robes Provided at Tourist Mosques
arrivalSteps.description: Major tourist mosques like Masjid Putra provide robes and headscarves at the entrance for visitors.

arrivalSteps.title: Remove Shoes
arrivalSteps.description: All footwear must be removed before entering any mosque.

arrivalSteps.title: Non-Muslims in Non-Prayer Areas Only
arrivalSteps.description: Non-Muslims are usually welcome in designated visitor areas but must not enter active prayer halls.

fallbackAlerts.message: Women must cover hair and wear a long robe inside all mosques. These are provided free at major tourist mosques.
During Ramadan, eating or drinking in public during daylight is an offence under Syariah law in many Malaysian states.

phrases.english: Is this mosque open to visitors?
phrases.nativeScript: Adakah masjid ini terbuka kepada pelawat?
phrases.romanized: Adakah masjid ini terbuka kepada pelawat?

scenarios.description: Etiquette at Chinese and Hindu temples

arrivalSteps.title: Remove Shoes at Entrances
arrivalSteps.description: Footwear must be removed before entering any Hindu or Buddhist temple.

arrivalSteps.title: Dress Modestly
arrivalSteps.description: Shorts and sleeveless tops are generally not acceptable. Long pants or skirts are preferred.

fallbackAlerts.message: During Thaipusam, Batu Caves is extremely crowded. Dress conservatively and be respectful of devotees carrying kavadis.

phrases.nativeScript: Bolehkah saya masuk ke kuil ini?
phrases.romanized: Bolehkah saya masuk ke kuil ini?

scenarios.description: Street food, bargaining, and hawker culture

arrivalSteps.title: Bargaining is Acceptable
arrivalSteps.description: Gentle bargaining is common at markets. Fixed-price stalls usually display price tags.

fallbackAlerts.message: Many stalls in Malaysia are halal-certified. Look for the JAKIM halal logo if dietary compliance is important to you.

phrases.english: Can you give me a discount?
